evals.prompt: hey i write a weekly newsletter about AI and design and every time i start a new issue i have to re-explain my whole voice and format to claude. can you help me make a skill so it just knows how i write? i like short punchy sentences, no fluff, and i always end with a 'one thing to try this week' section
evals.expected_output: A well-structured SKILL.md for a newsletter writing skill that captures voice, format, sections, and the signature closing section
tests: Does the output include a proper SKILL.md with frontmatter?
Does it capture the voice preferences (short, punchy, no fluff)?
Does it include the 'one thing to try this week' section?
Is the description pushy enough to trigger reliably?
Does it ask clarifying questions or just generate?
Is the language accessible to a non-developer?

evals.prompt: I need a skill that takes a messy Notion database export (CSV) and generates a formatted project status report as a .docx file. It should group tasks by status (done, in progress, blocked), calculate completion percentages, and flag anything that's been 'in progress' for more than 2 weeks. I use this every Friday for my team standup.
evals.expected_output: A technical SKILL.md with clear workflow steps, file processing instructions, formatting specs, and possibly bundled scripts
tests: Does it include step-by-step workflow instructions?
Does it specify how to handle the CSV parsing?
Does it mention grouping by status and calculating percentages?
Does it flag the 2-week 'in progress' items?
Does it reference scripts/ for deterministic tasks?
Does it specify .docx output format?

evals.prompt: turn this into a skill - we just spent the last hour figuring out how to analyze customer feedback. the process was: 1) read the CSV of feedback, 2) categorize each entry into themes (UX, pricing, features, support), 3) score sentiment 1-5, 4) generate a summary report with top themes and example quotes, 5) output as markdown. you kept getting the categories wrong until i told you to look for specific keywords per category
evals.expected_output: A SKILL.md extracted from conversation context with the workflow steps, the keyword-based categorization fix, and the output format
evals.persona: Product manager who iterated with Claude
tests: Does it follow the conversation-to-skill extraction path?
Does it incorporate the correction (keyword-based categorization)?
Does it include all 5 workflow steps?
Does it specify the categories (UX, pricing, features, support)?
Does it define the sentiment scoring scale?
Does it specify markdown output with themes and example quotes?

evals.prompt:
i have this skill that's supposed to help me write linkedin posts but it keeps triggering when i ask claude to write emails too. also the tone is way too formal, i want it more casual and real. can you fix it? here's my current SKILL.md:

---
name: linkedin-writer
description: Writes professional content for social media platforms.
---

# LinkedIn Writer

## Instructions

Write professional LinkedIn posts.

### Tone
Use a professional, corporate tone. Include industry terminology.

### Format
- Opening hook
- 3-4 paragraphs
- Call to action
- Hashtags
evals.expected_output: An improved SKILL.md that fixes the triggering (more specific description that excludes email), shifts tone from corporate to casual, preserves the original name, and demonstrates understanding of the update workflow
evals.persona: Frustrated user with a broken skill — will push back if suggestions feel off
tests: Preserves original skill name 'linkedin-writer'?
Fixes the description to exclude email triggers?
Shifts tone guidance from corporate to casual?
Includes negative trigger examples (what NOT to trigger for)?
Follows the update workflow (copy to writable location, etc.)?
Acknowledges the user's frustration constructively?

evals.prompt: i want claude to help me with something but i'm not sure exactly what to call it. basically every time i get a new client project i have to set up a bunch of stuff and it takes forever. like files and folders and things. can you make that faster somehow?
evals.expected_output: Claude should NOT immediately generate a skill. Instead, it should use the wizard to draw out what 'set up a bunch of stuff' actually means — what kind of projects, what files/folders, what tools, what's the deliverable. The skill should only be generated after clarifying questions are answered.
tests: Does NOT immediately generate a SKILL.md?
Asks what kind of projects/clients?
Asks what specific files and folders get created?
Asks what tools are involved (Figma, Google Drive, local folders)?
Uses plain language, no jargon?
Explains what a skill is in accessible terms?

evals.prompt: Build me a skill for processing bank statement PDFs. It needs to: 1) extract all transactions from the PDF using OCR if needed, 2) categorize each transaction (groceries, dining, transport, subscriptions, etc.), 3) calculate monthly totals per category, 4) detect anomalies — any transaction that's 3x the average for its category, 5) output a clean CSV and a markdown summary. This needs to be rock solid because I run it on 6 months of statements at once. Bundle whatever scripts make sense — I don't want Claude reinventing the wheel every time.
evals.expected_output: A SKILL.md with bundled scripts for PDF extraction, categorization, and anomaly detection. Should include scripts/ directory guidance, error handling for OCR failures, and the full pipeline as deterministic code rather than LLM-generated each run.
evals.persona: Technical power user who explicitly wants scripts and pushes back on LLM-only approaches
tests: Recommends bundled scripts in scripts/ directory?
Separates deterministic tasks (math, CSV output) from LLM tasks (categorization)?
Includes anomaly detection logic (3x average)?
Handles OCR failure edge cases?
Specifies both CSV and markdown output formats?
Addresses batch processing (6 months at once)?

evals.prompt: We use Notion as our project hub and every time a new sprint starts, someone has to manually create the sprint page from a template, add all the tasks from our backlog database, tag the team members, and set the dates. Can you make a skill that does this through Claude's Notion connection? We use the MCP integration already.
evals.expected_output: An MCP-enhanced skill that guides Claude on how to use Notion MCP tools in a specific sprint setup workflow. Should reference MCP capabilities, include Notion-specific patterns, and add the guidance layer on top.
evals.persona: Engineering manager with MCP already connected
tests: Recognizes this as an MCP enhancement skill pattern?
References MCP/Notion integration in the instructions?
Includes Notion-specific concepts (databases, pages, properties)?
Provides step-by-step workflow for sprint creation?
Handles the case where MCP connection fails or is unavailable?
Description triggers on sprint/Notion-related phrases?

evals.prompt: I want a skill that writes really detailed, thorough technical documentation but also I need it to be super concise and scannable. Like, comprehensive but short. And make sure it covers every edge case but don't make it too long. Oh and it should work for both API docs and user guides — same skill.
evals.expected_output: Claude should recognize the contradictions, surface them diplomatically, and help the user resolve the tension rather than producing a confused skill that tries to do everything. Should suggest either two skills or a skill with mode switching.
tests: Identifies the contradiction (thorough vs concise)?
Surfaces the tension diplomatically without being condescending?
Proposes a resolution (two modes, two skills, or progressive detail)?
Doesn't just produce a confused skill that ignores the conflict?
Handles the API docs vs user guide scope question?
Asks which constraint matters more to break the tie?

evals.prompt: can you make me a skill that just adds 'sent from my AI assistant' at the bottom of every email claude writes for me? that's literally all i want, nothing else
evals.expected_output: A minimal, tight skill — NOT an over-engineered monstrosity. The wizard should recognize this is simple and not force the user through 8 steps. The SKILL.md should be short.
evals.persona: User who wants something dead simple
tests: Produces a short, minimal SKILL.md (under 30 lines)?
Doesn't over-engineer with unnecessary sections?
Doesn't force the full wizard flow on a trivial request?
Description is appropriately scoped to email writing?
Correctly implements the signature append behavior?
Respects the user's 'that's literally all i want' boundary?

evals.prompt: Our design team (8 people) needs a skill for creating design critique documents. When someone submits a design for review, the reviewer should be able to ask Claude to generate a structured critique following our team's framework: 1) First impressions, 2) Alignment with design principles (we have 5), 3) Accessibility check, 4) Specific actionable feedback, 5) Summary recommendation (ship/iterate/rethink). This needs to work the same way for everyone on the team. How do we set this up?
evals.expected_output: A skill designed for team deployment with consistent output across 8 users. Should address team distribution, mention admin deployment, and ensure the critique framework is rigid enough for consistency but flexible enough for different design types.
evals.persona: Design lead setting up a team workflow
tests: Addresses team deployment (admin can deploy to workspace)?
Produces a rigid critique framework for consistency across reviewers?
Includes all 5 framework sections with guidance per section?
Asks about the team's 5 design principles to embed them?
Mentions that all 8 people will get the same skill behavior?
Handles different design types (UI, branding, illustration)?

evals.prompt:
Here's my SKILL.md draft, can you review it and tell me what's wrong? I've been using it for a week and it works OK but sometimes the output is inconsistent.

---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews code.
---

# Code Reviewer

Review code that the user shares. Look for bugs, performance issues, and style problems. Give feedback in a numbered list.

Be thorough but not nitpicky. Focus on things that actually matter.

If the code is in Python, check for PEP 8 compliance. If it's JavaScript, check for modern syntax usage.

Always end with a summary: how many issues found, severity breakdown (critical/warning/info), and an overall quality score from 1-10.
evals.expected_output: Should skip the wizard entirely and jump straight to review/improve mode. Should identify specific problems: thin description, no examples, vague 'thorough but not nitpicky', no output format template, missing edge cases. Should improve the skill without rewriting from scratch.
evals.persona: Developer with existing draft — wants review, not a rebuild
tests: Skips the wizard and goes straight to review mode?
Identifies the thin description as problem #1?
Points out vague instructions ('thorough but not nitpicky')?
Suggests adding an output format example?
Preserves the user's existing structure and intent?
Offers to run test cases after improvements?

evals.prompt: I want a skill for summarizing articles. Like when I paste a long article, Claude should give me a summary with key points and a one-line takeaway.
evals.expected_output: This is tricky — Claude already summarizes articles well without any skill. The skill creator should recognize this overlap and help the user understand when a skill adds value vs when Claude's default behavior is sufficient. If the user has specific formatting or style requirements, those justify a skill. If not, it might not be worth creating one.
evals.persona: User requesting a skill that overlaps with built-in Claude ability
tests: Recognizes overlap with Claude's built-in summarization?
Asks what's different from what Claude already does?
Probes for specific format/style requirements that would justify a skill?
Doesn't dismiss the user or refuse to create the skill?
If user has specifics, creates the skill willingly?
If user has no specifics, gently explains Claude already does this?
